query: effect of fluoride replacing hydroxyl vs calcium/phosphate ions in hydroxyapatite on crystal strength

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Several challenges exist regarding the application of hydroxyapatite in medicine. For instance, using hydroxyapatite as an implant presents inherent defects and fine porosity that can act as crack initiators. Following crack initiation, propagation can lead to catastrophic failure during use. Additionally, the application of bulk hydroxyapatite may result in a modulus mismatch between the bone and the implant, leading to disproportionate load sharing. [10] Conversely, hydroxyapatite, irrespective of its source, always contains trace elements such as fluoride ions (F - ) and hydroxyl ions (OH - ), which contribute to increased crystallite size and decreased solubility, thereby enhancing the strength of the apatite. [19] At the same time, elements such as phosphide ions (PO 3 3- ) and chloride ions (Cl - ) have been shown to weaken the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite by reducing crystallite size and increasing solubility. [10] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The phase composition and preparation method influence the chemical stability of hydroxyapatite. For instance, substituting apatite with magnesium, carbonate, or strontium increases solubility. [17] In contrast, substituting fluoride reduces solubility. Sintered hydroxyapatite has higher chemical stability than non-sintered forms, making it less soluble in vivo. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fluor (F) must be present in the right place and at the right time to interfere with demineralization and remineralization events. However, even low ppm values are sufficient to fulfill its effect. When pH drops up to 4.5, fluorapatite (FA) is formed while hydroxyapatite (HA) is dissolved. If F is present, the enamel dissolution decreases because the enamel recovers those lost minerals as fluorapatite. Furthermore, the action of F is complemented by its natural effect on remineralization, enhancing the redeposition of calcium and phosphate present in the biofilm fluid when the pH rises. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wet method produces nonstoichiometric hydroxyapatite powder that may contain impurities such as hydrogen phosphate, carbonate, chloride, and sodium ions. These impurities can lead to the formation of calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite. [1] Previous studies have identified these impurities as uncontrollable variables that can significantly alter the crystallographic arrangement and chemical properties of hydroxyapatite, ultimately affecting its dissolution process.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, fluoride incorporated into the enamel crystal structure at the tooth developmental stages does not have a sufficient effect on the acid solubility of enamel. [9] [10] However, the fluoride present in the biofilm due to regular topical exposure surrounds the carbonated apatite crystals (enamel crystals) effectively inhibiting tooth demineralization. At the time of bacterial acid production, the fluoride in the plaque fluid travels down with the acid into the enamel subsurface, adsorbs into its crystal structure, and protects it against dissolution. Fluorapatite Ca 10 (PO 4 ) 6 F 2 is an enamel mineral much more resistant to acid dissolution than other tooth minerals (carbonated apatite and hydroxyapatite). Fluorapatite results from replacing the OH ion in hydroxyapatite with a fluoride ion. [8] Fluoride promotes remineralization

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite are influenced by factors such as phase composition, crystal size, and synthesis method. Pure hydroxyapatite typically has a bending strength of 38 to 250 MPa, compressive strength of 120 to 150 MPa, and tensile strength of 38 to 300 MPa. The Young modulus ranges from 35 to 120 GPa, depending on the presence of impurities. [19] Meanwhile, the Weibull modulus, ranging from 5 to 18, indicates hydroxyapatite is a brittle material. To enhance its mechanical properties, tough hydroxyapatite can be obtained by incorporating TCP, which increases its flexural strength. In contrast, the flexural strength reaches its minimum when hydroxyapatite contains calcium oxide CaO. [10] [19] Furthermore, the sintering temperature also significantly alters the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite. Higher sintering temperatures lead to increased density, compressive strength, grain size, and torsional strength. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite from natural sources is commonly fabricated from materials such as fishbone, coral, bovine bone, eggshell, and seashells through a calcination process. Hydroxyapatite produced from these natural sources is often nonstoichiometric due to the presence of trace ions. [13] [17] These traces of ions, which include cations such as Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Sr 2+ , Zn 2+ , and Al 3+ , as well as anions such as F - , Cl - , SO 4 2- , and CO 3 2- , are beneficial for promoting rapid bone regeneration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, the solid-state reaction yields a stoichiometric and well-defined crystalline structure of hydroxyapatite. However, this method requires high temperatures and extended heat treatment procedures. The raw materials used in the solid-state method should have a Ca/P ratio of 1.67 and must be ball-milled to ensure uniform particle size. [2] The solid-state reaction method solely depends on the solid diffusion of ions into the raw materials, thereby necessitating temperatures around 1250 ºC to initiate the reaction. [2] Moreover, prolonged heat treatment transforms singular crystalline particles into more blocky crystals, and the increase in crystalline size leads to a decrease in porosity, which is associated with the aging process. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Several methods exist for producing hydroxyapatite from synthetic or natural sources. Synthetic hydroxyapatite is derived from raw materials such as calcium carbonate, calcium hydroxide, calcium nitrate, diammonium hydrogen phosphate, and ammonium hydroxide. The fabrication process of hydroxyapatite involves both wet methods and solid-state reactions, followed by calcination or sintering. These methods utilize chemical reactions that adjust the content of CaO and TCP to achieve the stoichiometric conditions necessary for hydroxyapatite formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Dentin is less hard than enamel but still harder than bone and is composed of weight 70% mineralized material, 20% organic material, and 10% water. The crystals mainly consist of calcium hydroxyapatite Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2 and small percentages of carbonate and fluoride. Dentin protects the pulp and has great tensile strength.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite is an inorganic mineral with a typical apatite lattice structure represented as (A10(BO4)6C2), where A, B, and C correspond to Ca, PO 4 , and OH, respectively. Pure hydroxyapatite contains 39.68% calcium and 18% phosphorus by weight, resulting in a Ca/P molar ratio of 1.67. Commercial hydroxyapatite products have Ca/P ratios that can be higher or lower than 1.67. This variation in the Ca/P ratio indicates a phase shift between tricalcium phosphate (TCP) and calcium oxide (CaO). Hydroxyapatite with a Ca/P ratio greater than 1.67 contains more CaO than TCP, while those with a lower ratio contain more TCP. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: The calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, sodium fluoride combination is linked to the compound known in Czech as fluorid sodný, indicating that this multi‑ingredient formulation is derived from or mapped from fluorid sodný. Fluorid sodný itself is identified as having the specific form sodium fluoride F‑18, a radioactive isotope of sodium fluoride. Consequently, the combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride can be understood as a preparation that incorporates the properties of sodium fluoride F‑18, reflecting its origin from fluorid sodný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: The combination product containing calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is conceptually derived from the sodium‑fluoride entity (fluorid sodný). Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the sodium‑fluoride group, are described as having a fluoride (fluoridy) relationship, and are identified as a type of “other mineral supplements in ATC,” the same ATC category that includes selenium supplement. Within the sodium‑fluoride classification, a specific form exists known as sodium fluoride F‑18. Together, these relationships show how various mineral‑based formulations—ranging from multi‑component calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride to single‑ingredient fluoride and selenium supplements—are organized under the broader ATC grouping of other mineral supplements, highlighting their shared pharmacological context and classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1827
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a therapeutic formulation that incorporates kalcium-glycerolfosfát and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components. Kalcium-glycerolfosfát provides a calcium‑phosphate source that can support mineralization processes, while sodium hyaluronate contributes viscoelastic and lubricating properties useful for tissue repair and joint function. By combining these two agents, the replacement preparation is designed to enhance clinical outcomes in procedures that require both structural support and improved biomechanical performance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that include several distinct calcium salts. Among these are calcium glycerophosphate, which is a calcium‑based glycerophosphate compound, calcium acetate, a calcium salt of acetic acid, and tricalcium phosphate, a calcium phosphate used in various medical and nutritional contexts. All three entities share calcium as a central component, linking them together as members of the broader category of calcium compounds that are relevant for applications such as phosphate binding, calcium supplementation, and bone health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1043
retrieved_docs.content: for reflux‑related gastrointestinal disorders, while sodium monofluorophosphate provides the fluoride activity in a wide range of oral care products. Understanding these interconnections assists clinicians, pharmacists and formulators in product selection, anticipation of excipient effects, and appropriate therapeutic use of calcium carbonate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2047
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that encompass specific entities such as calcium acetate and kalcium‑glycerolfosfát. Both calcium acetate and kalcium‑glycerolfosfát are individual members of the broader category of Calcium Compounds, sharing the common characteristic of containing calcium and often being considered in medical or nutritional contexts where calcium supplementation or calcium‑based reactions are relevant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific types of calcium‑containing mineral supplement products used to support nutritional needs.
